Asian ETF markets closed 2025 with a clear shift toward equity exposure, underpinned by China’s late-year rally and steady momentum in Japan. This improved equity backdrop drove strong inflows into China’s broad market benchmarks, with more than $13 billion allocated to CSI A500 Index ETFs during December. The China Southern and Huatai PineBridge products led gains, reflecting renewed investor confidence in China’s policy support and prospects for growth recovery.

At the same time, investors reduced cash and duration exposure, signalling a move away from capital preservation strategies. Money market ETFs saw notable redemptions, with the Kodex Money Market ETF recording net outflows of approximately $1.3 billion, while bond markets softened modestly amid rising inflation expectations.

Overall, December flows pointed to a constructive shift in risk appetite across Asia-Pacific, as investors favoured large-cap equity exposure and trimmed defensive allocations heading into 2026. Summary of Monthly Flows

December closed the year with investors using ETFs to adjust portfolios as confidence grew around an upcoming shift in monetary policy. As inflation eased and expectations for rate cuts strengthened, investors reduced defensive positions and increased exposure to broad equity markets, while trimming allocations to cash-like products and long-duration bonds.

Flow patterns varied by region but shared a more constructive tone. In the US, strong inflows reflected confidence in corporate earnings, with investors favouring large-cap benchmark ETFs. In Europe, investors selectively added equity exposure, particularly in financials and broad-market ETFs, supported by improving balance sheets and funding conditions. In Asia-Pacific, flows were more concentrated, led by China equity ETFs as expectations for policy support increased, alongside steady participation in other regional markets.

Overall, December flows reinforced the role of ETFs as efficient and flexible allocation tools, allowing investors to adjust regional and asset-class exposures while positioning for a more supportive policy environment in 2026.

data.title: What Earnings Explain, And What They Don't: Insights From 150 Years Of Market Data
data.description:
By Terri Campbell, CFA

Stock prices and corporate earnings move closely together over long horizons, a relationship confirmed by more than a century of data compiled by Robert Shiller. This analysis examines the strength of that long-term linkage and tests whether changes in the earnings–price correlation offer insight into future stock market returns.

The results show that while earnings help explain market behavior over time, fluctuations in the correlation itself do not provide a useful basis for forecasting returns. The sections that follow document empirical patterns across multiple rolling periods and assess the limits of using correlation measures as market-timing tools. The findings may also help financial advisors frame long-term market behavior for clients in a grounded and intuitive way.

What This Analysis Aims to Clarify

I examine the long-term relationship between stock prices and corporate earnings for two main reasons.

First, the findings offer a straightforward way to explain stock market behavior over long investment horizons. I define a long horizon as more than 10 years, which is a useful minimum timeframe for retirement planning and for making asset allocation decisions.

Second, after calculating the correlations between prices and earnings, I tested whether changes in the correlation over time might serve as a leading indicator of future returns. Specifically, I asked whether periods of unusually low historical correlation were followed by stronger or weaker subsequent stock market performance.

Correlation Results

The analysis uses monthly averages of the S&P Composite earnings per share and the S&P Composite price. The reported monthly earnings, stock price, and returns data for the S&P Composite companies are based on Shiller’s data from 1871 through December 2024.

Across multiple time periods, the correlations between earnings and prices were consistently high.

Time Period Correlation
Full data set (01/1871 – 12/2024) 0.977
100 years (01/1925 – 12/2024) 0.974
Post-1940 Investors Act (08/1940 – 04/2024) 0.973
50 Years (01/1975 – 12/2024) 0.963

I chose common time periods to examine the data and note the following:

  • One starting point is the 1940 Investors Act, used to test whether results differed after investor protections and more uniform accounting standards were introduced. The difference appears negligible.
  • The past 10- and 20-year periods were included to reflect what is often considered a typical retirement-planning horizon.

Correlation Changes Over Time

The correlation between earnings and stock prices does fluctuate over time, particularly across shorter horizons such as the five-, 10-, and 20-year windows. The rolling 50-year correlations also vary, though within a much narrower range.

The lowest rolling 50-year correlation occurred during the first half of the 20th century, when the data series reached 0.6. Given the backdrop of two world wars, the Great Depression, and limited market regulation prior to 1940, it is notable that the correlation did not fall further.

Variability increased as the time horizon shortened. In the rolling 20-year series, correlations fell below 0.50 for a full decade between February 1918 and December 1928, and again briefly in December 1948.

The rolling 10-year correlations fell below zero during three periods: at the end of World War I and World War II, and during the high inflation era of the late 1970s and early 1980s.

Rolling five-year correlations naturally showed the most volatility, with deeper drops and more frequent swings, including multiple periods of negative correlation. Both the average and median rolling five-year correlations were lower than those observed over longer horizons.

Does the Variability in Correlations Correspond with Returns?

To test whether variation in the earnings–price correlation has any predictive value for stock returns, we ran regressions of correlation levels against subsequent annualized returns.

The R² between S&P Composite earnings and price from 1871 through 2024 is very high at 0.95. Given the strength of this long-term relationship—and the relative rarity of low-correlation periods—it is reasonable to ask whether those periods might function as buy or sell signals. In other words, does variation in the earnings–price correlation help predict future returns?

I evaluated this question across multiple rolling time horizons. The resulting R² values — linking correlation levels to subsequent annualized returns — were far lower than the R² between earnings and price themselves. For the rolling 10-year and five-year windows, the R² fell close to zero, indicating virtually no predictive relationship.

The rolling 50-year period showed the strongest relationship with a R2 of 0.53.

For the rolling 20-year windows, the R² was 0.24, reflecting considerably more variability.

Variability increased further in the rolling 10-year series, where the R² fell to 0.06.

The rolling five-year periods show no consistent pattern. R2 is nearly 0.0 (actual: 1.27E-07).

Overall, I found no evidence that changes in the earnings–price correlation predict future annualized returns. The data show that the two measures do not move together in any meaningful way for horizons shorter than 50 years.

Predictive Power of Correlation

The strong long-term relationship between earnings and prices offers a clear explanation for the rise and fall of stock markets over extended periods. It provides a simple and intuitive framework for understanding long-run equity trends.

However, the second goal – determining whether changes in the correlation could serve as a predictive measure for annualized returns – was not achieved. The evidence suggests that other factors beyond the earnings–price relationship drive the rate of change in annualized returns, even though the two series move closely together over long horizons.

Key Takeaways

  • Earnings and stock prices move closely together over long horizons. More than 150 years of Shiller data show a consistently strong relationship between the two series.
  • Shorter windows introduce substantial noise. Correlations fluctuate meaningfully over five-, 10-, and 20-year periods, reflecting wars, inflation shocks, and structural changes.
  • Correlation strength does not imply predictive power. Shifts in the earnings–price correlation have little ability to forecast subsequent returns at horizons relevant to most investors.
  • Only the longest windows show limited explanatory power. Even the 50-year regressions, with an R² of 0.53, offer only modest insight, while shorter horizons fall close to zero.

Earnings help explain long-term market behavior, but they do not help time the market.

Disclaimer: Please note that the content of this site should not be construed as investment advice, nor do the opinions expressed necessarily reflect the views of CFA Institute.

data.title: Lanxess Is Slogging Through A Painful Downturn, But The Cycle Will Recover Eventually
data.description:
Whatever progress LANXESS (LNXSF) (LNXSY) (LXSG.DE) had been making in convincing the Street that it had turned a corner with its operations and capability to maintain through-the-cycle profits is basically gone now. The macro environment went from bad to worse throughout 2025, and an influx of cheap imports drove utilization to levels that cannot sustain attractive profits and cash flow generation for this large German chemicals company.

LANXESS' shares are down about 40% since my last update (the ADRs have done a little better due to currency), meaningfully underperforming a weak chemicals sector that has broadly seen the same macro challenges, demand evaporation, and export competition, with only some respite from lower input costs.

It’s tempting to argue that conditions can’t get much worse for LANXESS, and I do think it’s noteworthy that in a terrible year that has seen utilization fall below 70% (typically 85% is where margins really inflect positively), EBITDA margin is still likely to come in at the high single digits and the company could still generate some positive free cash flow. On the other hand, next year is likely to be almost as challenging, and any value here may require a line of sight to volume growth to surface.

A Painful Year… And No Obvious Respite In Sight

As I said in the open, 2025 was a terrible year. I outlined some concerns I had about the macro environment early in the year, but pretty much all that could have gone wrong did go wrong.

Agriculture (around 20% of the business) was poor throughout most of the year, with weaker commodity prices making affordability an issue and export competition from Asia vying for what demand there was. Energy industry demand was likewise soft throughout much of the year, while autos and construction weakened as the year went on.

All told, somewhere in the neighborhood of 66% to 75% of LANXESS’s revenue base saw meaningful weakness throughout 2025, and while food and consumer goods performed somewhat better, there wasn’t much real growth there, and as I discussed in a recent article on Innospec (IOSP), there was widespread margin pressure in consumer-related products due to input cost pressure and minimal pricing power.

With buyers worried about the macro environment and reportedly only buying for immediate needs (as opposed to buying multiple months’ worth at a time), volumes plunged. While LANXESS started the year off okay with 2% volume growth, that reversed to 4% contraction in Q2 and over 6% contraction in Q3, and given a +3% comp for the upcoming fourth quarter, I’m not expecting good news here.

These weak volumes have driven utilization down to 67%, and it’s tough for any chemical company to generate attractive margins at that level.

Unfortunately it doesn’t look like a V-shaped recovery is in sight. German chemicals group VCI is looking for industry volumes to decline another 3.5% in 2026, and that is likely to put even more pressure on utilization and margins. I do expect some recovery in construction in the second half (helped in part by German fiscal stimulus and lower rates in the U.S.), but agriculture and autos are likely to remain pressured, and I don’t see other end-markets like chemicals or energy picking up that slack.

Monetizing Envalior Will Help… But It May Take A While

LANXESS announced in late September that it had started the process to monetize its 40% stake in the Envalior JV. Under the terms of the JV, Advent is obligated to acquire LANXESS’s stake at a 12x EBITDA multiple, implying a value of around EUR 1.2B against a roughly EUR 3.9B EV for LANXESS today.

There are some important “buts” to the monetization process. First, it’s not a given that LANXESS will be able to sell the entire stake and may only be able to sell half. Second, even that may not happen—the sale process is subject to Advent securing financing on acceptable terms, and given the challenges in the chemicals industry today, that may not be possible. LANXESS does have the right to sell the stake on April 1, 2028 irrespective of financing, but it may well prove to be the case that LANXESS has to wait another two years to monetize this asset—certainly the market’s valuation would suggest that to be the case.

Self-Help Can Only Do So Much

A terrible 2025 has cast a shadow over the progress that LANXESS has been making. The company has successfully diversified its business, reducing its former overreliance on the auto and tire industries, and has exited some less attractive segments like leather chemicals and synthetic rubber. Management has also been taking costs out of the business and restructuring, selling, or exiting businesses that it can’t bring up to adequate levels of performance.

LANXESS has also increased its global diversification, adding more exposure to North America and benefitting from lower feedstock costs there, and has generally reduced its cyclicality and increased exposure to markets with longer-term attractive characteristics (including agriculture, consumer goods, and electronics).

As 2025 shows, though, those moves can only help so much when demand disappears and foreign exporters dump bulk chemicals they can’t use into the global market.

Management is moving forward with another cost reduction program (worth around EUR 100M) in addition to previously-announced process and production efficiency improvement efforts. Cutting costs by EUR 100M isn’t trivial next to around EUR 500M in expected EBITDA, and management is moving to close inefficient plants in the face of excess capacity.

Other positive drivers could offer some incremental upside over the next 12-24 months, including a more stable tariff situation, German fiscal stimulus working its way into the economy, and anti-dumping actions against imports. None of those will replace underlying demand improvement, though, and that is not likely to develop until the second half of 2026 at best.

The Outlook

Weak pricing and volumes have been a one-two punch to revenue, and where I was once looking for low single-digit revenue growth, it’s now looking like LANXESS will see a roughly 10% year-over-year decline in revenue. Weak utilization (from typical levels in the high 70%s to the high 60%s now) has magnified the pain on the EBITDA line, with full-year margin likely to slip below 9% instead of the 11% I’d previously expected.

Looking ahead, I don’t expect a dramatic demand improvement in 2026, as I think that second-half recovery will have to start from a lower base given ongoing weakness in the first half of the year. Said differently, LANXESS will be paddling hard in 2026 just to stay in basically the same place. I do expect a more meaningful recovery in 2027, though, and I do think 3% to 4% long-term revenue growth beyond that is achievable (and likely well above that in particularly strong recovery years when there is limited spare capacity and more pricing power).

When it comes to cyclical companies, it’s tempting to see both the good times and the bad times as the new normal, and many sell-side analysts are modeling LANXESS as if margins will never meaningfully recover where they were once expecting improvement into the low-to-mid-teens (for EBITDA) on the basis of those cost efficiency and business diversification moves. I do think this has been a painful reset for the story, but I do think EBITDA margin can get back above 10% when the business recovers and ultimately see expansion toward the low-to-mid teens.

That, in turn, should push free cash flow margins back toward the 3%-4% range over the next five years and ultimately drive around 6% long-term FCF growth.

Discounting those cash flows back suggests around 30% upside today, and I get a similar result with a 7.5x multiple on my FY’26 EBITDA estimate. While that is above the long-term average forward EBITDA multiple, that’s appropriate in a cyclical trough, and I get a similar result with a 6.5x multiple (in line with the long-term average) on my estimate of normalized full-cycle EBITDA.

The Bottom Line

Roughly 30% upside would normally argue for a positive stance on the shares, and that’s before factoring in the potential impact of the Envalior stake monetization (if it happens in 2026). Still, I’m hesitant to argue for buying these shares when volumes are still so weak, the outlook is not particularly encouraging, and management’s estimates keep coming down. This is a name I intend to watch more closely, though, as I do see worthwhile upside whenever this cyclical downturn peters out and a recovery cycle begins.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
